sentences.chinese: 卷六十四下 嚴朱吾丘主父徐嚴終王賈傳 第三十三下
sentences.literal: Volume 64b: Biographies of Yan, Zhu, Wuqiu, Zhufu, Xu, Yan, Zhong, Wang, and Jia, Part Two, Number 33b.
sentences.idiomatic: Volume 64b, Part Two: biographies of Yan An, Zhu Mai Chen, Wuqiu Shouwang, Zhufu Yan, Xu Le, Yan Cang, Zhong Jun, Wang Bao, and Jia Yi—the thirty-third set of biographies, lower scroll.

sentences.chinese: 嚴安者，臨菑人也。
sentences.literal: Yan An was a man of Linzi.
sentences.idiomatic: Yan An came from Linzi.

sentences.chinese: 以故丞相史上書，曰：
sentences.literal: In the capacity of a former clerk of the chancellor, he submitted a memorial, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Serving as a clerk who had once worked under the chancellor, he presented a memorial that began:

sentences.chinese: 臣聞鄒衍曰：「政教文質者，所以云救也，當時則用，過則舍之，有易則易也，故守一而不變者，未睹治之至也。」
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard Zou Yan say: 'Government and instruction, culture and substance—these are means by which one speaks of remedy; what fits the times is employed, what has passed is set aside; when there is something to change, one changes it; therefore one who holds fast to one thing and does not alter has never seen the utmost of good order.'
sentences.idiomatic: I have heard Zou Yan say that policy and moral teaching, refinement and plainness, exist to redress imbalance: use what suits the moment, discard what is out of date, and change when change is due. A ruler who clings to a single formula and never revises it has never grasped what true good government looks like.

sentences.chinese: 今天下人民用財侈靡，車馬衣裘宮室皆競修飾，調五聲使有節族，雜五色使有文章，重五味方丈於前，以觀欲天下。
sentences.literal: Now the people of the realm in their use of wealth are extravagant; carriages, horses, garments, furs, and palaces all vie in ornament; they tune the five tones so that they have rhythm, mix the five colors so that they have pattern, pile the five flavors a full square before them, in order to display desire to the realm.
sentences.idiomatic: Today the common people spend money with reckless luxury: they outdo one another in decking out carriages, horses, robes, furs, and houses; they arrange the five notes into elaborate music, blend the five colors into rich designs, and heap rare flavors across a table before them—all to flaunt appetite and set a fashion for the whole world.

sentences.chinese: 彼民之情，見美則願之，是教民以侈也。
sentences.literal: As for the disposition of the people, when they see beauty they desire it; this is teaching the people extravagance.
sentences.idiomatic: Human nature being what it is, people covet whatever looks splendid; in effect, the court is schooling them in excess.

sentences.chinese: 侈而無節，則不可贍，民離本而徼末矣。
sentences.literal: If there is extravagance without restraint, then it cannot be supplied; the people leave the root and pursue the branch.
sentences.idiomatic: Extravagance without limits outruns any purse, and the people abandon farming to chase petty profit at the margins.

sentences.chinese: 末不可徒得，故搢紳者不憚為詐，帶劍者夸殺人以矯奪，而世不知媿，故姦軌浸長。
sentences.literal: The branch cannot be obtained in vain; therefore girdled gentlemen do not shrink from deceit; sword-bearing men boast of killing people in order to seize by force, and the age does not know shame; therefore villainy gradually grows.
sentences.idiomatic: Those secondary gains do not come honestly, so belted officials turn to fraud without a qualm, and men who wear swords swagger through murder and robbery, while society feels no shame. Crime and violence spread unchecked.

sentences.chinese: 夫佳麗珍怪固順於耳目，故養失而泰，樂失而淫，禮失而采，教失而偽。
sentences.literal: Now beauties and rare curiosities indeed please ear and eye; therefore nurturing misses and becomes excessive, music misses and becomes dissolute, ritual misses and becomes ornamental, instruction misses and becomes false.
sentences.idiomatic: Splendid sights and rare treasures naturally delight the senses, yet when we indulge them, nourishment slides into excess, music into licentiousness, ritual into mere display, and moral teaching into hollow pretense.

sentences.chinese: 偽、采、淫、泰，非所以範民之道也。
sentences.literal: Falsehood, ornament, dissoluteness, excess—these are not the way to model the people.
sentences.idiomatic: Pretense, ornament, debauchery, and extravagance are no model for how the people ought to live.

sentences.chinese: 是以天下人民逐利無已，犯法者眾。
sentences.literal: Therefore the people of the realm pursue profit without end, and those who violate the law are many.
sentences.idiomatic: The result is a tireless scramble for gain across the land, and the jails fill with lawbreakers.

sentences.chinese: 臣願為民制度以防其淫，使貧富不相燿以和其心。
sentences.literal: Your servant wishes to institute regulations for the people in order to guard against their excess, so that poor and rich do not dazzle one another and thereby harmonize their hearts.
sentences.idiomatic: I urge you to set norms that curb excess, so that rich and poor are not locked in mutual envy and hearts can find peace.

sentences.chinese: 心既和平，其性恬安。
sentences.literal: When the heart is already harmonious and peaceful, their nature is tranquil and secure.
sentences.idiomatic: When minds are at peace, temperaments grow calm and steady.

sentences.chinese: 恬安不營，則盜賊銷；
sentences.literal: If tranquil and secure they do not scheme, then bandits and thieves disappear.
sentences.idiomatic: Content people who are not driven by restless ambition will not turn to theft.

sentences.chinese: 盜賊銷，則刑罰少；
sentences.literal: If bandits and thieves disappear, then punishments are few.
sentences.idiomatic: With theft rare, punishments become infrequent.

sentences.chinese: 刑罰少，則陰陽和，四時正，風雨時，草木暢茂，五穀蕃孰，六畜遂字，民不夭厲，和之至也。
sentences.literal: If punishments are few, then yin and yang are harmonious, the four seasons are correct, wind and rain come in season, plants and trees flourish luxuriantly, the five grains ripen abundantly, the six kinds of livestock breed as they should, and the people do not die young from pestilence—this is the utmost of harmony.
sentences.idiomatic: Fewer punishments mean balanced yin and yang, orderly seasons, timely rain and wind, lush growth, abundant harvests, thriving herds, and no plague of untimely deaths—this is harmony brought to perfection.

sentences.chinese: 臣聞周有天下，其治三百餘歲，成康其隆也，刑錯四十餘年而不用。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that when Zhou possessed the realm, its government was more than three hundred years; Cheng and Kang were its height, and punishments were set aside for more than forty years without being used.
sentences.idiomatic: I am told the Zhou held the realm for over three hundred years of rule; the reigns of King Cheng and King Kang were its high point, when criminal penalties lay unused for more than forty years.

sentences.chinese: 及其衰，亦三百餘年，故五伯更起。
sentences.literal: When it came to its decline, it was also more than three hundred years; therefore the Five Hegemons arose in succession.
sentences.idiomatic: Its decline lasted another three hundred years, a span in which the Five Lords Protector rose one after another.

sentences.chinese: 伯者，常佐天子興利除害，誅暴禁邪，匡正海內，以尊天子。
sentences.literal: The hegemons constantly assisted the Son of Heaven in raising benefit and removing harm, punishing violence and prohibiting evil, rectifying within the seas, in order to honor the Son of Heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: Those hegemons habitually aided the Son of Heaven by advancing good, removing harm, crushing cruelty, and checking wickedness, bringing order within the Four Seas so as to uphold the throne.

sentences.chinese: 五伯既沒，賢聖莫續，天子孤弱，號令不行。
sentences.literal: When the Five Hegemons were already gone, worthy and sage men did not continue; the Son of Heaven was isolated and weak, and commands did not go forth.
sentences.idiomatic: After the last hegemon died, no worthy successor appeared; the Zhou king grew isolated and feeble, and his decrees carried no weight.

sentences.chinese: 諸侯恣行，彊陵弱，眾暴寡。
sentences.literal: The feudal lords acted as they pleased; the strong encroached on the weak, the many tyrannized the few.
sentences.idiomatic: Regional lords did as they pleased: the strong swallowed the weak, and great states bullied small ones.

sentences.chinese: 田常篡齊，六卿分晉，並為戰國，此民之始苦也。
sentences.literal: Tian Chang usurped Qi, the six ministers divided Jin, and together they became the Warring States—this was the beginning of the people's suffering.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian Chang seized Qi, six noble clans carved up Jin, and the age of the Warring States began—the first great wave of misery for the common people.

sentences.chinese: 於是彊國務攻，弱國修守，合從連衡，馳車轂擊，介冑生蟣蝨，民無所告愬。
sentences.literal: Thereupon strong states devoted themselves to attack, weak states cultivated defense; vertical and horizontal alliances; racing chariots, hub striking hub; armor and helmets bred lice; the people had nowhere to lodge complaints.
sentences.idiomatic: Mighty states threw everything into conquest while weaker ones dug in behind their walls; leagues shifted from north–south pacts to east–west deals; chariots jammed wheel-hub to wheel-hub; soldiers lived so long in mail that lice bred in the lining; ordinary folk had no court to which they could appeal.

sentences.chinese: 及至秦王，蠶食天下，并吞戰國，稱號皇帝，一海內之政，壞諸侯之城。
sentences.literal: When it reached the King of Qin, he nibbled away the realm, swallowed the Warring States, styled himself emperor, unified government within the seas, and destroyed the cities of the feudal lords.
sentences.idiomatic: Then came the First Emperor of Qin, who gnawed away the states one by one, swallowed the warring kingdoms, took the title of emperor, centralized all authority, and razed the walls of the old feudatories.

sentences.chinese: 銷其兵，鑄以為鍾虡，示不復用。
sentences.literal: He melted their weapons and cast them into bells and bell-stands, showing that they would not be used again.
sentences.idiomatic: He had their bronze weapons melted down and recast into bells and bell-frames, a public pledge that arms would not be taken up again.

sentences.chinese: 元元黎民得免於戰國，逢明天子，人人自以為更生。
sentences.literal: The black-haired common people were able to escape the Warring States period; they encountered a bright enlightened Son of Heaven; each person thought himself reborn.
sentences.idiomatic: The common people, delivered at last from endless war, believed they had met an enlightened sovereign; every household felt as though it had been given a new life.

sentences.chinese: 鄉使秦緩刑罰，薄賦斂，省繇役，貴仁義，賤權利，上篤厚，下佞巧，變風易俗，化於海內，則世世必安矣。
sentences.literal: If formerly Qin had relaxed punishments, lightened taxes and levies, reduced corvée labor, honored benevolence and rightness, despised power and profit, made superiors honest and thick, and inferiors not crafty and clever, changed customs and altered practices, and transformed within the seas, then generation after generation would surely have been secure.
sentences.idiomatic: Had Qin only eased its penalties, cut taxes and labor service, honored humanity and duty over ruthless calculation, encouraged integrity in high places and discouraged sycophancy below, and reformed customs throughout the realm, its peace might have lasted for generations.

sentences.chinese: 秦不行是風，循其故俗，為知巧權利者進，篤厚忠正者退，法嚴令苛，諂諛者眾，曰聞其美，章廣心逸。
sentences.literal: Qin did not practice this wind; it followed its old customs; those who were clever in power and profit advanced, those who were honest, thick, loyal, and upright retreated; laws were strict and orders harsh; flatterers were many; daily they heard praise of its beauty; splendor was broad and the heart was dissipated.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin did nothing of the kind. It clung to old habits, promoting the cunning and the self-seeking while sidelining the steadfast and true. Statutes grew harsher, orders more cruel, and flatterers multiplied; the court daily heard only its own praises until ambition swelled and discipline collapsed.

sentences.chinese: 欲威海外，使蒙恬將兵以北攻彊胡，辟地進境，戍於北河，飛芻輓粟以隨其後。
sentences.literal: Desiring to awe beyond the seas, he sent Meng Tian leading troops to attack northward the strong Hu, opened land and advanced the border, garrisoned at the Northern River, and flew fodder and hauled grain to follow behind.
sentences.idiomatic: Intent on striking terror into lands beyond China, he sent Meng Tian north against the Xiongnu, pushed the frontier forward, planted garrisons along the northern river line, and then rushed grain and fodder north in an endless train to supply them.

sentences.chinese: 又使尉屠睢將樓船之士攻越，使監祿鑿渠運糧，深入越地，越人遁逃。
sentences.literal: He also sent Commandant Tu Sui leading tower-ship troops to attack Yue, sent Supervisor Lu to dig a canal to transport grain, deeply entered Yue territory, and the Yue people fled.
sentences.idiomatic: He also ordered Commandant Tu Sui to take marines in tower ships against the Yue, while Superintendent Lu cut a canal to move supplies deep into Yue country until the local people melted away into the hills.

sentences.chinese: 曠日持久，糧食乏絕，越人擊之，秦兵大敗。
sentences.literal: Days dragged on and time was prolonged; grain supplies were exhausted; the Yue people struck them, and Qin troops were greatly defeated.
sentences.idiomatic: The campaign dragged on until provisions ran out; the Yue struck back and shattered the Qin army.

sentences.chinese: 秦乃使尉佗將卒以戍越。
sentences.literal: Qin then sent Commandant Tuo leading troops to garrison Yue.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin then dispatched Commandant Zhao Tuo with a garrison to hold the conquered south.

sentences.chinese: 當是時，秦禍北構於胡，南挂於越，宿兵於無用之地，進而不得退。
sentences.literal: At this time, Qin's disaster was constructed north with the Hu and hung south with Yue; it lodged troops in useless places, advancing but unable to retreat.
sentences.idiomatic: At that moment Qin was caught between the Xiongnu in the north and Yue in the south, with armies tied down in barren country where they could advance but not withdraw.

sentences.chinese: 行十餘年，丁男被甲，丁女轉輸，苦不聊生，自經於道樹，死者相望。
sentences.literal: For more than ten years it went on; adult males donned armor, adult females transported supplies; suffering, they had no means to live; they hanged themselves on trees along the roads; the dead faced one another.
sentences.idiomatic: For over a decade every able-bodied man wore armor and every woman hauled supplies for the front. Life became unbearable; people strung themselves up from roadside trees until the corpses lined the highways.

sentences.chinese: 及秦皇帝崩，天下大畔。
sentences.literal: When the Qin emperor died, the realm greatly rebelled.
sentences.idiomatic: When the First Emperor died, the empire erupted in revolt.

sentences.chinese: 陳勝、吳廣舉陳，武臣、張耳舉趙，項梁舉吳，田儋舉齊，景駒舉郢，周市舉魏，韓廣舉燕，窮山通谷，豪士並起，不可勝載也。
sentences.literal: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang raised Chen; Wu Chen and Zhang Er raised Zhao; Xiang Liang raised Wu; Tian Dan raised Qi; Jing Ju raised Ying; Zhou Shi raised Wei; Han Guang raised Yan; through exhausted mountains and connecting valleys, bold men rose together—their number cannot be fully recorded.
sentences.idiomatic: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang seized Chen, Wu Chen and Zhang Er raised Zhao, Xiang Liang rose in Wu, Tian Dan in Qi, Jing Ju in Ying, Zhou Shi in Wei, Han Guang in Yan—bold leaders sprang up in every ravine and pass, too many to list.

sentences.chinese: 然本皆非公侯之後，非長官之吏，無尺寸之勢，起閭巷，杖棘矜，應時而動，不謀而俱起，不約而同會，壤長地進，至乎伯王，時教使然也。
sentences.literal: Yet originally none were descendants of dukes or marquises, none were clerks of senior officials, none had the force of a foot or an inch of territory; they rose from lanes and alleys, leaned on thorn staffs and spear-shafts, moved in response to the times, rose together without planning, assembled without covenant, extended territory and advanced land, reaching to hegemon-kings—it was the teaching of the times that made it so.
sentences.idiomatic: Yet none of these men sprang from ducal houses or high office; they held not an inch of legitimate power. They came out of the back alleys with improvised weapons, stirred by the moment, acting without prior conspiracy yet rising as one, seizing ground until some stood as kings or hegemons—such was the lesson the age had taught them.

sentences.chinese: 秦貴為天子，富有天下，滅世絕祀，窮兵之禍也。
sentences.literal: Qin was honored as Son of Heaven and rich with the realm; its generations were extinguished and sacrifices cut off—the disaster of exhausted arms.
sentences.idiomatic: Though Qin had worn the imperial crown and owned the wealth of the world, its line was extinguished and its ancestral rites cut short—this is what endless war buys a dynasty.

sentences.chinese: 故周失之弱，秦失之彊，不變之患也。
sentences.literal: Therefore Zhou lost through weakness, Qin lost through strength—the misfortune of not changing.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhou fell because it grew too weak to act; Qin fell because it clung too fiercely to force. Both perished from the same refusal to change course.

sentences.chinese: 今徇南夷，朝夜郎，降羌僰，略薉州，建城邑，深入匈奴，燔其龍城，議者美之。
sentences.literal: Now you display the southern Yi, have Yelang court, reduce Qiang and Bo, seize the foul region, build cities and towns, deeply enter the Xiongnu, burn their Dragon City—discussers praise it.
sentences.idiomatic: Today the court campaigns in the southern tribes, brings Yelang to audience, subdues Qiang and Bo peoples, seizes border prefectures, founds new towns, drives deep into the steppe, and burns the Xiongnu court at Longcheng—and advisers call all of it glorious.

sentences.chinese: 此人臣之利，非天下之長策也。
sentences.literal: This is benefit to ministers, not the long plan for the realm.
sentences.idiomatic: That may serve ambitious officials; it is no lasting policy for the empire.

sentences.chinese: 今中國無狗吠之警，而外累於遠方之備，靡敝國家，非所以子民也。
sentences.literal: Now the central states have no alarm of dogs barking, yet outwardly are burdened with preparations in distant places, wasting and exhausting the state—this is not the way to treat the people as children.
sentences.idiomatic: The heartland faces no threat that would set village dogs barking, yet we strain the treasury on garrisons thousands of miles away. Bleeding the state dry is no way to care for the people as a father should.

sentences.chinese: 行無窮之欲，甘心快意，結怨於匈奴，非所以安邊也。
sentences.literal: Pursuing desires without end, gratifying the heart with pleasure, tying resentment with the Xiongnu—this is not the way to secure the border.
sentences.idiomatic: To indulge boundless appetite for glory, savor short-term triumph, and pile up lasting hatred with the Xiongnu is no recipe for a quiet frontier.

sentences.chinese: 禍挐而不解，兵休而復起，近者愁苦，遠者驚駭，非所以持久也。
sentences.literal: Disaster grasps and does not loosen; troops rest and rise again; those near are anxious and bitter, those far are frightened—this is not the way to endure long.
sentences.idiomatic: Calamity clings without release; each pause in fighting is followed by a new mobilization. Those nearby groan under the burden; distant peoples tremble at the rumor of arms. No dynasty can last on such a footing.

sentences.chinese: 今天下鍛甲摩劍，矯箭控弦，轉輸軍糧，未見休時，此天下所共憂也。
sentences.literal: Now the realm hammers armor and polishes swords, straightens arrows and draws bowstrings, transports and conveys military grain—no time of rest is seen; this is what the realm jointly worries about.
sentences.idiomatic: Across the empire men beat mail, hone blades, straighten shafts, and string bows, while grain trains roll east and west without cease. This is the specter that haunts every household.

sentences.chinese: 夫兵久而變起，事煩而慮生。
sentences.literal: When arms are long, changes arise; when affairs are numerous, cares are born.
sentences.idiomatic: Protracted war breeds mutiny; tangled administration breeds fresh crises.

sentences.chinese: 今外郡之地或幾千里，列城數十，形束壤制，帶脅諸侯，非宗室之利也。
sentences.literal: Now the lands of outer commanderies are sometimes nearly a thousand li, arrayed cities are several tens; form binds and soil restrains, girdling and threatening the feudal lords—it is not benefit to the imperial house.
sentences.idiomatic: Some frontier commanderies sprawl across a thousand li and hold dozens of walled towns whose geography can choke rival powers as a belt tightens the waist. That strength lies outside the imperial clan, and it bodes ill for the throne.

sentences.chinese: 上觀齊晉所以亡，公室卑削，六卿大盛也；
sentences.literal: Looking up, observe how Qi and Jin perished—the ducal house was humble and cut down, the six ministers greatly flourished.
sentences.idiomatic: Consider why Qi and Jin fell: their ruling houses withered while great ministerial clans grew too strong to curb.

sentences.chinese: 下覽秦之所以滅，刑嚴文刻，欲大無窮也。
sentences.literal: Looking down, survey why Qin was destroyed—punishments were harsh and writing was carved, desires were great without end.
sentences.idiomatic: Look lower, at Qin's ruin: savage law, pitiless edicts, and ambition without limit.

sentences.chinese: 今郡守之權非特六卿之重也，地幾千里非特閭巷之資也，甲兵器械非特棘矜之用也，以逢萬世之變，則不可勝諱也。
sentences.literal: Now the power of a commandery governor is not merely the weight of the six ministers; land of nearly a thousand li is not merely the capital of lanes and alleys; armor, weapons, and implements are not merely the use of thorn staffs and spear-shafts—if one meets the changes of ten thousand generations, then it cannot be fully concealed.
sentences.idiomatic: A modern governor commands more real authority than any of Jin's six ministers; his domain is vaster than a rebel's alley-kingdom; his arsenals hold more than thornwood spears. Should the world shift again, the danger is too obvious to gloss over.

sentences.chinese: 後以安為騎馬令。
sentences.literal: Later, because of this, An was made Master of Horse.
sentences.idiomatic: Afterward Yan An was appointed Master of the Imperial Horse.

sentences.chinese: 終軍字子雲，濟南人也。
sentences.literal: Zhong Jun, courtesy name Ziyun, was a man of Jinan.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun, whose courtesy name was Ziyun, came from Jinan.

sentences.chinese: 少好學，以辯博能屬文聞於郡中。
sentences.literal: In youth he loved learning; by eloquence, breadth, and ability to compose texts he was heard of in the commandery.
sentences.idiomatic: From boyhood he loved study and won countywide renown for wide reading, sharp debate, and a ready pen.

sentences.chinese: 年十八，選為博士弟子。
sentences.literal: At age eighteen, he was selected as a disciple of the erudites.
sentences.idiomatic: At eighteen he was chosen as a student of the imperial academy.

sentences.chinese: 至府受遣，太守聞其有異材，召見軍，甚奇之，與交結。
sentences.literal: When he arrived at the office to receive dispatch, the grand administrator heard that he had unusual talent, summoned and met Jun, greatly marveled at him, and formed ties with him.
sentences.idiomatic: When he went to the yamen for his travel orders, the grand administrator heard reports of his gifts, called him in, was deeply impressed, and struck up a friendship.

sentences.chinese: 軍揖太守而去，至長安上書言事。
sentences.literal: Jun bowed to the grand administrator and departed; he reached Chang'an and submitted a memorial discussing affairs.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun bowed once to the governor and left for the capital, where he filed a memorial on state policy.

sentences.chinese: 武帝異其文，拜軍為謁者給事中。
sentences.literal: Emperor Wu marveled at his writing and appointed Jun Herald and Palace Attendant.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wu was struck by the quality of his prose and named him court herald with standing access to the inner palace.

sentences.chinese: 從上幸雍祠五畤，獲白麟，一角而五蹄。
sentences.literal: He followed the emperor in visiting Yong to sacrifice at the five altars; they obtained a white unicorn, one horn and five hoofs.
sentences.idiomatic: He accompanied the sovereign to Yong for the sacrifices at the five shrines, where hunters brought in a white unicorn bearing a single horn and five hooves.

sentences.chinese: 時又得奇木，其枝旁出，輒復合於木上。
sentences.literal: At the same time they also obtained a strange tree; its branches came out sideways and then again joined together on the tree.
sentences.idiomatic: About the same time someone presented a freakish tree whose limbs jutted sideways only to fuse back into the trunk.

sentences.chinese: 上異此二物，博謀群臣。
sentences.literal: The emperor marveled at these two things and broadly consulted the ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor regarded both prodigies as extraordinary and asked his officials for wide-ranging counsel.

sentences.chinese: 軍上對曰：
sentences.literal: Jun submitted a reply, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun responded with a memorial that read:

sentences.chinese: 臣聞詩頌君德，樂舞后功，異經而同指，明盛德之所隆也。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that the Odes praise the lord's virtue, music and dance extol the later achievement; different classics but the same aim, clarifying where abundant virtue rises.
sentences.idiomatic: The Book of Odes hymns a ruler's virtue; ritual music celebrates deeds handed down from sage-kings. The texts differ, yet they point the same way: they mark where true greatness gathers.

sentences.chinese: 南越竄屏葭葦，與鳥魚群，正朔不及其俗。
sentences.literal: Southern Yue hides among reeds and rushes, flocks with birds and fish; the correct beginning of the year does not reach their customs.
sentences.idiomatic: The Yue huddle beyond the reeds like waterfowl and fish; our calendar and rites have never touched their ways.

sentences.chinese: 有司臨境，而東甌內附，閩王伏辜，南越賴救。
sentences.literal: When officials approached the border, Eastern Ou submitted within, the king of Min submitted to guilt, and Southern Yue relied on rescue.
sentences.idiomatic: As soon as imperial officers reached the frontier, Eastern Ou came in, the king of Min accepted punishment, and Yue itself owed its survival to Han mercy.

sentences.chinese: 北胡隨畜薦居，禽獸行，虎狼心，上古未能攝。
sentences.literal: The northern Hu follow livestock and shift dwelling; they act like birds and beasts, have hearts like tigers and wolves; high antiquity could not subdue them.
sentences.idiomatic: The northern nomads drift with their herds like animals, with appetites as fierce as wolves'. Even the sage-kings of high antiquity could not fully tame them.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍秉鉞，單于奔幕；
sentences.literal: The great general grasped the axe; the Chanyu fled to the desert curtain.
sentences.idiomatic: The supreme commander took the war-axe in hand and drove the Chanyu headlong into the steppe.

sentences.chinese: 票騎抗旌，昆邪右衽。
sentences.literal: The Piaoqi general raised the banner; Kunye wore the right lapel.
sentences.idiomatic: The Swift Cavalry general unfurled his standard until Prince Hunye surrendered and adopted Han dress.

sentences.chinese: 是澤南洽而威北暢也。
sentences.literal: This is grace south reaching and might north extending.
sentences.idiomatic: Thus kindness has soaked the south while awe runs clear across the north.

sentences.chinese: 若罰不阿近，舉不遺遠，設官俟賢，縣賞待功，能者進以保祿，罷者退而勞力，刑於宇內矣。
sentences.literal: If punishments do not favor the near, promotions do not omit the far, offices are set up awaiting the worthy, rewards are hung awaiting merit, the able advance to preserve salary, the incompetent retire to labor with strength—then there is model within the cosmos.
sentences.idiomatic: When penalties spare no favorite, promotions reach the humblest talent, posts await true worth, honors hang ready for merit, capable men rise to secure their stipends while the inept step down to manual work, the whole realm becomes your mirror of justice.

sentences.chinese: 履眾美而不足，懷聖明而不專，建三宮之文質，章厥職之所宜，封禪之君無聞焉。
sentences.literal: Treading many beauties yet not sufficing, embracing sagely brightness yet not monopolizing, establishing the pattern and substance of the three palaces, clarifying what suits each duty—of lords who performed feng and shan, none are heard of thus.
sentences.idiomatic: You walk amid every excellence yet still feel wanting; you hold sagely insight yet refuse to monopolize it; you set the three palaces in proper balance of ornament and plainness and define each office's charge. No ruler who merely mounted Mount Tai for feng and shan rites ever matched that.

sentences.chinese: 夫人命初定，萬事草創，及臻六合同風，九州共貫，必待明聖潤色，祖業傳於無窮。
sentences.literal: When the mandate of man is first fixed, the myriad things are begun; when it reaches the six directions sharing one wind and the nine provinces sharing one strand, one must await a bright sage to polish, and the ancestral enterprise is transmitted to infinity.
sentences.idiomatic: At the founding of a house all institutions are rough; only when the six quarters breathe a single culture and the nine regions run on one thread does a truly enlightened sovereign put the finishing polish on them, so the patrimony may pass down without end.

sentences.chinese: 故周至成王，然後制定，而休徵之應見。
sentences.literal: Therefore Zhou reached King Cheng, and afterward regulations were fixed, and the response of auspicious signs appeared.
sentences.idiomatic: The house of Zhou did not complete its statutes until the reign of King Cheng; only then did propitious omens begin to answer its virtue.

sentences.chinese: 陛下盛日月之光，垂聖思於勒成，專神明之敬，奉燔瘞於郊宮，獻享之精交神，積和之氣塞明，而異獸來獲，宜矣。
sentences.literal: Your Majesty flourishes the light of sun and moon, hangs sagely thought on carved completion, devotes reverence to spirits, offers burning and burying at the suburban palace; the essence of offerings and feasting intersects spirits, accumulated harmony qi fills brightness, and strange beasts come to be captured—it is fitting.
sentences.idiomatic: Your Majesty outshines sun and moon, bends sage reflection toward perfecting the record, concentrates reverence on the gods, and presents burnt and buried offerings at the suburban shrines until the subtle ether of sacrifice mingles with the spirits and a tide of harmony fills the bright sky. Small wonder strange beasts should present themselves.

sentences.chinese: 昔武王中流未濟，白魚入於王舟，俯取以燎，群公咸曰「休哉！」
sentences.literal: Formerly King Wu, midstream not yet crossed, a white fish entered the king's boat; he bent and took it to burn as offering; the dukes all said, 'Auspicious!'
sentences.idiomatic: Once, when King Wu had not yet finished fording the river, a white fish leaped into his barge; he lifted it out for a burnt offering, and the nobles cried, 'Excellent omen!'

sentences.chinese: 今郊祀未見於神祇，而獲獸以饋，此天之所以示饗，而上通之符合也。
sentences.literal: Now suburban sacrifice has not yet been seen by the spirits, yet a beast was obtained to present as offering—this is what Heaven uses to show feasting, and the tally of communication on high.
sentences.idiomatic: You have not yet announced the suburban rites to the high gods, yet Heaven sends a beast fit for the altar—clear proof that the spirits accept your worship and that your virtue resonates above.

sentences.chinese: 宜因昭時令日，改定告元，苴以白茅於江淮，發嘉號于營丘，以應緝熙，使著事者有紀焉。
sentences.literal: It is fitting to follow the bright season and auspicious day, change and fix the proclamation of the first year, wrap with white rushes at the Yangzi and Huai, issue a fine title at Yingqiu, in order to respond to bright progress, so that those who record affairs have a record.
sentences.idiomatic: Seize an auspicious season and day to proclaim a new era name, invest the princes along the Yangzi and Huai with bundles of white rushes, and broadcast a glorious title from Yingqiu, echoing the bright mandate so historians may set it down in good order.

sentences.chinese: 蓋六鶂退飛，逆也；
sentences.literal: As for six wild geese flying backward—it is contrary.
sentences.idiomatic: When six wild geese beat their way upwind, the Spring and Autumn Annals read it as ill omen.

sentences.chinese: 白魚登舟，順也。
sentences.literal: The white fish ascending the boat is compliant.
sentences.idiomatic: The white fish leaping into the royal boat betokened favor from Heaven.

sentences.chinese: 夫明闇之徵，上亂飛鳥，下動淵魚，各以類推。
sentences.literal: As for the signs of brightness and darkness, above they disturb flying birds, below they move deep fish—each is inferred by category.
sentences.idiomatic: Omens of light and shadow work by kind: Heaven stirs the birds aloft, and the fish below feel it too.

sentences.chinese: 今野獸并角，明同本也；
sentences.literal: Now wild beasts share horns—brightness is the same root.
sentences.idiomatic: A beast in the wild with twin horns merged as one proclaims that all creatures share a single root.

sentences.chinese: 眾支內附，示無外也。
sentences.literal: Many branches attach within—showing there is nothing outside.
sentences.idiomatic: When every branch people turns inward to the throne, it signals that nothing lies beyond the emperor's embrace.

sentences.chinese: 若此之應，殆將有解編髮，削左衽，襲冠帶，要衣裳，而蒙化者焉。
sentences.literal: Responses like this—perhaps there will be those who undo braided hair, cut the left lapel, put on cap and belt, gird robe and skirt, and receive transformation.
sentences.idiomatic: Portents such as these foretell tribes who will unbind their hair, cut away barbarian dress, and don caps, sashes, robes, and skirts to receive your civilization.

sentences.chinese: 斯拱而俟之耳！
sentences.literal: This is folding hands and awaiting it!
sentences.idiomatic: You have only to fold your hands and wait.

sentences.chinese: 對奏，上甚異之，由是改元為元狩。
sentences.literal: When the reply was presented, the emperor greatly marveled at it; thereby the era name was changed to Yuanshou.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor was so struck by the memorial that he renamed the reign Yuanshou.

sentences.chinese: 後數月，越地及匈奴名王有率眾來降者，時皆以軍言為中。
sentences.literal: Several months later, in Yue territory and among the Xiongnu famous kings there were those who led crowds to come surrender; at the time all took Jun's words as hitting the mark.
sentences.idiomatic: Within a few months Yue chiefs and noted Xiongnu princes began leading their followers in to surrender—just as Zhong Jun had predicted.

sentences.chinese: 元鼎中，博士徐偃使行風俗。
sentences.literal: In the Yuanding period, Erudite Xu Yan was sent to tour customs.
sentences.idiomatic: During the Yuanding era the court scholar Xu Yan was dispatched to inspect local customs.

sentences.chinese: 偃矯制，使膠東、魯國鼓鑄鹽鐵。
sentences.literal: Yan forged an edict, causing Jiaodong and the state of Lu to smelt and cast salt and iron.
sentences.idiomatic: Xu Yan forged imperial orders and had Jiaodong and Lu cast iron and boil salt on their own.

sentences.chinese: 還，奏事，徙為太常丞。
sentences.literal: Returning, he reported affairs and was transferred to be assistant to the Chamberlain for Ceremonials.
sentences.idiomatic: On his return he filed his report and was promoted to assistant director under the Chamberlain for Ceremonials.

sentences.chinese: 御史大夫張湯劾偃矯制大害，法至死。
sentences.literal: Imperial Counselor Zhang Tang impeached Yan for forging an edict with great harm; by law it reached death.
sentences.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Zhang Tang impeached him for counterfeiting orders with grave consequences—a capital crime.

sentences.chinese: 偃以為春秋之義，大夫出疆，有可以安社稷，存萬民，顓之可也。
sentences.literal: Yan thought that according to the meaning of the Spring and Autumn Annals, when a grandee goes beyond the border, if there is something by which he can secure the altars of soil and grain and preserve the myriad people, to assume authority is permissible.
sentences.idiomatic: Xu Yan cited the Spring and Autumn principle that a minister abroad may act on his own authority if the altars and the lives of the people hang in the balance.

sentences.chinese: 湯以致其法，不能詘其義。
sentences.literal: Tang by this applied the law but could not refute his meaning.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Tang could press the statute but could not overturn the moral argument.

sentences.chinese: 有詔下軍問狀，軍詰偃曰：「古者諸侯國異俗分，百里不通，時有聘會之事，安危之勢，呼吸成變，故有不受辭造命顓己之宜；
sentences.literal: There was an edict sent down to Jun to ask the circumstances; Jun interrogated Yan, saying: 'In antiquity the feudal states differed in custom and were divided; within a hundred li they did not communicate; from time to time there were matters of visiting and meeting; the posture of safety and danger could change in a breath; therefore there was propriety in not receiving orders and making commands, assuming authority for oneself.'
sentences.idiomatic: An edict ordered Zhong Jun to examine the case. He questioned Xu Yan: 'In ancient times each feudal state kept its own ways; a hundred li might as well have been a world apart. Envoys hurried between courts because fortune could turn in a breath, so a minister might rightly assume emergency powers without waiting for a written order.'

sentences.chinese: 今天下為一，萬里同風，故春秋『王者無外』。
sentences.literal: Now the realm is one, ten thousand li share one wind; therefore the Spring and Autumn Annals say "the king has nothing outside."
sentences.idiomatic: Today the empire is one house under a single wind for ten thousand li, and the Annals teach that the king knows no "outside."

sentences.chinese: 偃巡封域之中，稱以出疆何也？
sentences.literal: Yan toured within the sealed domain—calling it going beyond the border, how is that?
sentences.idiomatic: You never left the emperor's domain—how can you call that "crossing the border"?

sentences.chinese: 且鹽鐵，郡有餘臧，正二國廢，國家不足以為利害，而以安社稷存萬民為辭，何也？」
sentences.literal: Moreover as for salt and iron, the commanderies have surplus in store; whether or not the two kingdoms' monopolies were suspended would not materially help or harm the central government—so to cite securing the altars and preserving the myriad people as your excuse, how is that?
sentences.idiomatic: Besides, every commandery stockpiles salt and iron; ending the two kingdoms' local monopolies would barely touch the central treasury. What sense is there in pleading the fate of the altars and the people?

sentences.chinese: 又詰偃：「膠東南近琅邪，北接北海，魯國西枕泰山，東有東海，受其鹽鐵。
sentences.literal: He again interrogated Yan: 'Jiaodong on the south nears Langye, on the north joins Beihai; the state of Lu on the west pillows Mount Tai, on the east has the Eastern Sea—they draw on their own salt and iron.'
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong Jun pressed further: 'Jiaodong borders Langye to the south and Beihai to the north; Lu lies against Mount Tai in the west and the Eastern Sea in the east—both regions already draw salt and iron from their own ground.'

sentences.chinese: 偃度四郡口數田地，率其用器食鹽，不足以并給二郡邪？
sentences.literal: Yan estimated the four commanderies' population numbers and fields and lands; generally their tools and eating salt—was it not enough to supply together the two states?
sentences.idiomatic: If you reckoned the population and acreage of the four neighboring commanderies, could their usual tools and salt rations not have supplied both kingdoms?

sentences.chinese: 將勢宜有餘，而吏不能也？
sentences.literal: Or was the situation fitting to have surplus, yet the clerks could not?
sentences.idiomatic: Or was there plenty of capacity while the local officials simply failed to manage it?

sentences.chinese: 何以言之？
sentences.literal: By what is this said?
sentences.idiomatic: What proves that?

sentences.chinese: 偃矯制而鼓鑄者，欲及春耕種贍民器也。
sentences.literal: Yan forged an edict and smelted and cast because he wished to catch up with spring plowing and planting to supply the people's tools.
sentences.idiomatic: You forged orders to cast tools, you say, so peasants would have iron for the spring planting.
